risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "All invited hospitals participated in the collaborative, but they did so in two phases for purposes of detecting the effect of the collaborative on improvement. Once the first phase - which lasted 12 months - was over, all hospitals entered the collaborative. During the first phase, the hospitals that were awaiting entry acted as a control group for those already in the QIC." Comment: The report describes phased entry with a control period, but it does not state how hospitals were assigned to phase 1 versus phase 2, and it does not mention a random method (e.g. computer-generated sequence, random numbers, blocks, stratification). Therefore the sequence generation method is insufficiently described and risk is unclear rather than high.

Quote: "All invited hospitals participated in the collaborative, but they did so in two phases for purposes of detecting the effect of the collaborative on improvement. Once the first phase - which lasted 12 months - was over, all hospitals entered the collaborative. During the first phase, the hospitals that were awaiting entry acted as a control group for those already in the QIC." Comment: The report describes phased allocation of hospitals but does not state how the assignment to phase 1 versus waiting-list control was concealed from those enrolling/assigning hospitals. There is no explicit mention of a concealment mechanism such as central allocation or sequentially numbered, opaque, sealed envelopes. Therefore allocation concealment cannot be confirmed.

risk_of_bias.domain: Blinding of participants and personnel (performance bias)
risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "During the first phase, the hospitals that were awaiting entry acted as a control group for those already in the QIC." and "A package of support was available to participating hospitals from the Stroke 90:10 faculty, including executive mentoring visits, direct access to the project director and an improvement advisor, and weekly online sharing and learning sessions via a web based portal (extranet)." Comment: The comparison was between hospitals already receiving the collaborative and hospitals waiting to enter it, so participants and personnel necessarily knew whether they were in the intervention or control phase. Because the outcomes were process/quality-of-care indicators (e.g. compliance with stroke care bundle components), provider behaviour could plausibly be influenced by this knowledge.

Quote: "QIC participants were expected to collect monthly monitoring data and submit it to the faculty team." and "Those who took part in interviews included ... data collection staff ... reflecting the broad range of professionals involved in the QIC." Comment: Outcome assessment appears to have been done by participating hospital staff/data collectors within the intervention teams, not by an independent blinded assessor; these assessors would have known whether their hospital was in the collaborative phase. The outcomes were chart/process-based quality indicators, and at least some required judgement-dependent assessment from records (e.g. "Mood assessment (during the in-patient stay)" and "Documented evidence of MDT goals set for rehabilitation"). Because unblinded local staff assessed at least some judgement-dependent outcomes, knowledge of allocation could have influenced outcome measurement.

Quote: "All invited hospitals participated in the collaborative, but they did so in two phases for purposes of detecting the effect of the collaborative on improvement. Once the first phase - which lasted 12 months - was over, all hospitals entered the collaborative. During the first phase, the hospitals that were awaiting entry acted as a control group for those already in the QIC. Two hospitals withdrew from the first phase and one did not submit sufficient data for analysis, leaving nine hospitals. Similarly, sufficient data were available from nine hospitals in the second phase." Comment: This reports some post-allocation loss at the cluster level and suggests 9 hospitals per phase were analysed, but the paper does not clearly give complete per-arm randomized versus analysed numbers for the analysed outcomes, nor reasons for missing data in each comparison group. The observed loss appears potentially substantial, but without fully reported denominators/reasons by arm and no clear missing-data handling, the risk cannot be confidently judged Low; however, the reporting is also insufficient to establish High risk with positive evidence.
